On this page

Privacy & Ethics ​

The digital world has created unprecedented challenges for privacy. Every app you install, every website you visit, and every post you make leaves data about you in the hands of others. Understanding privacy rights, ethical responsibilities, and the legal framework is essential for every digital citizen.

Grade 11–12

Privacy, ethics, and social networking effects are Grade 11 content. POPIA, surveillance, and AI ethics are extended at Grade 12.

What is Privacy? ​

Privacy is the right of individuals to control how their personal information is collected, stored, used, and shared.

Personal information includes:

  • Full name, address, phone number, email
  • ID number, passport number
  • Financial information (bank account, credit card)
  • Health and medical records
  • Location data
  • Biometric data (fingerprints, face scan)
  • Online behaviour (browsing history, purchases)
  • Opinions, beliefs, political affiliation

The privacy paradox: People say they value privacy, but voluntarily share enormous amounts of personal data on social media and with apps.

Why Privacy Matters ​

ReasonExplanation
Protection from harmIdentity theft, fraud, and targeted harassment require personal data
AutonomyControl over your own narrative and how others see you
TrustConfidence that organisations handle data responsibly
Freedom of expressionPeople self-censor when they know they're being monitored
Political freedomSurveillance chills dissent and political activity

How Personal Data is Collected ​

SourceHowExamples
Social mediaUser input + behavioural trackingPosts, likes, location check-ins
Search enginesSearch history, click patternsGoogle search history
AppsLocation, contacts, microphone, cameraWeather app tracking location
CookiesBrowser tracking across sitesAdvertising cookies
IoT devicesSensor data from smart home devicesAlexa recording conversations
Loyalty programsPurchase historyPick n Pay Smart Shopper
CCTVFacial recognition from camerasPublic surveillance cameras
Data brokersAggregating from many sourcesSelling profile data to advertisers

Ethics in ICT ​

Ethics in ICT involves making morally responsible decisions about technology.

Key Ethical Principles ​

PrincipleDescription
Informed consentUsers should know what data is collected and agree to it
Data minimisationCollect only data that is necessary
TransparencyBe open about how data is used
FairnessDon't discriminate; don't use data to harm people
AccountabilityOrganisations responsible for breaches and misuse
Purpose limitationUse data only for the stated purpose

Ethical Issues in ICT ​

IssueEthical concern
Surveillance capitalismBusinesses profiting by collecting and selling personal data
Algorithmic biasAI systems that discriminate based on race, gender, age
DeepfakesAI-generated fake videos/images that deceive
AI decision-makingWho is responsible when an AI makes a harmful decision?
Digital manipulationSocial media algorithms designed to maximise engagement (outrage)
Facial recognitionSurveillance without consent in public spaces
Data retentionHow long should organisations keep personal data?

Social Networking and Its Effects ​

Positive Effects ​

EffectExplanation
CommunicationInstant, free communication with anyone globally
CommunityFinding others with shared interests
ActivismOrganising social movements, awareness campaigns
EducationSharing knowledge and learning resources
BusinessMarketing, networking, customer engagement
NewsReal-time information sharing

Negative Effects ​

EffectExplanation
Privacy erosionVast personal data harvested and sold
MisinformationFake news spreads faster than corrections
Echo chambersAlgorithms show content you agree with → reinforces existing views
CyberbullyingHarassment at scale, 24/7
AddictionVariable reward mechanisms keep users engaged (like gambling)
Social comparisonHighlight reels → unrealistic standards → anxiety, depression
RadicalisationExtremist content recommendations
Time theftExcessive screen time reduces productivity

South African Privacy Law — POPIA ​

POPIA (Protection of Personal Information Act, 2013, effective 2021) is South Africa's data protection law.

What POPIA covers: ​

  • Any organisation or individual processing personal information of South Africans
  • "Processing" includes collecting, storing, using, sharing, deleting data

8 Conditions for Lawful Processing ​

ConditionRequirement
AccountabilityResponsible party must ensure compliance
Processing limitationOnly process with consent or legitimate reason
Purpose specificationState purpose before collecting; use only for that purpose
Further processing limitationDon't use data for incompatible purposes
Information qualityData must be complete, accurate, and up to date
OpennessNotify data subject of collection; publish privacy policy
Security safeguardsProtect data from loss, damage, unauthorised access
Data subject participationIndividuals can access and correct their own data

Rights under POPIA: ​

  • Right to know what data is held about you
  • Right to access your personal data
  • Right to correct inaccurate data
  • Right to object to processing
  • Right to request deletion ("right to be forgotten")

POPIA penalties: ​

  • Fines up to R10 million
  • Imprisonment up to 10 years for serious offences
  • Regulatory body: Information Regulator

GDPR (European equivalent) ​

GDPR (General Data Protection Regulation, 2018) is the EU equivalent of POPIA.

FeatureGDPR
CoverageAny organisation handling EU residents' data
Max fine€20 million or 4% of global annual turnover
ConsentMust be freely given, specific, informed, unambiguous
Right to deletion"Right to be forgotten"

GDPR set the global standard — POPIA is modelled on it.

Digital Rights and Responsibilities ​

Rights ​

  • Right to access information
  • Right to privacy
  • Right to be forgotten (deletion of personal data)
  • Right to know how your data is used
  • Freedom from surveillance and discrimination

Responsibilities ​

  • Respect others' privacy — don't share private information
  • Think before posting — impact on others' dignity
  • Report illegal content (child exploitation, hate speech)
  • Verify information before sharing (stop misinformation)
  • Respect intellectual property (no piracy)
  • Use technology ethically

Artificial Intelligence — Ethical Considerations ​

IssueDetail
BiasAI trained on biased data produces biased outputs — e.g. facial recognition performs worse on darker skin tones
AccountabilityWho is responsible when AI makes a wrong decision (self-driving car accident)?
Transparency"Black box" AI — decisions cannot be explained
Job displacementAutomation replacing human workers
PrivacyAI systems require massive data collection
DeepfakesConvincing fake media threatens truth and trust
WeaponisationAI in autonomous weapons systems

Key Terms ​

TermDefinition
PrivacyRight to control how personal information is collected and used
Personal informationAny data that identifies or can identify a person
POPIASouth African Protection of Personal Information Act (2013)
GDPREU General Data Protection Regulation (2018)
Informed consentAgreement based on full understanding of what is being agreed to
Data minimisationCollecting only data that is necessary
Digital footprintTrail of data left by online activity
Algorithmic biasAI producing unfair results due to biased training data
Echo chamberOnline environment exposing people only to opinions they already hold
Surveillance capitalismBusiness model based on collecting and selling personal data

Exam Focus ​

High-Frequency Questions

  1. "What is POPIA? What does it aim to protect?" — Protection of Personal Information Act; aims to protect individuals' personal information from misuse by organisations; sets conditions for lawful processing

  2. "Give TWO rights that POPIA grants to individuals" — right to access own data; right to correct inaccurate data; right to object to processing; right to request deletion

  3. "Give TWO negative effects of social media on society" — spread of misinformation; cyberbullying; echo chambers reinforce existing views; addiction by design; privacy erosion; social comparison causing anxiety

  4. "What is algorithmic bias? Give an example." — AI systems that produce unfair outcomes due to biased training data; example: facial recognition that works less accurately on darker skin tones

  5. "Explain the concept of informed consent in the context of data collection" — Users must know specifically what data is collected, how it will be used, and who it will be shared with, and must actively agree to this before data is collected
